subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) PHP runtime builtin: the implementation of PHP's preg_quote() string function, which escapes characters that carry special meaning in a PCRE pattern (and optionally a caller-supplied delimiter) so an arbitrary string can be embedded literally in a regular expression. It processes a userland-supplied PHP string and returns a new escaped HHVM String.
provenance: Identified by the HHVM runtime String API used throughout: str.data(), str.size(), String::charAt(), the String(int, ReserveString) reserve constructor, String::mutableData(), and String::setSize() are the hphp/runtime/base type-string interface. The function name preg_quote and the char-class switch (regex metacharacters plus a caller delimiter) match HHVM's ext_preg preg_quote. The in-code tag /* sample: primevul_validation_0000_vuln */ and the filename train_0222_cwe190.cpp are dataset metadata (treated as untrusted labels, not relied upon); the judgement below is made blind on the code itself.
trust_boundary: preg_quote is a PHP userland builtin. Both parameters cross the trust boundary: `str` is a fully attacker-controlled PHP string (its bytes AND its length are chosen by whoever calls preg_quote($x) in PHP), and `delimiter` is likewise caller-controlled. The hostile inputs are (1) the LENGTH of `str` — an attacker can make str.size() arbitrarily large up to the runtime's string-size limit — and (2) the content bytes of `str`, which select how many output bytes each input byte expands to. The output buffer size is computed from the attacker-controlled length, so the length is the primary hostile field.

what_the_code_does.code:
String preg_quote(const String& str,
                  const String& delimiter /* = null_string */) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. It takes an HHVM String `str` (the text to be escaped) by const reference and an optional `delimiter` String (defaulting to the null string) that, if non-empty, names one extra character to escape beyond the fixed regex metacharacter set. It returns a new String containing the escaped text. Passing by const reference means both source strings stay alive for the whole call, so pointers derived from them remain valid until return.

what_the_code_does.code:
const char* in_str = str.data();
  const char* in_str_end = in_str + str.size();
what_the_code_does.explanation: `in_str` is a raw pointer to the first byte of str's internal buffer, and `in_str_end` is a one-past-the-end pointer computed as base + str.size(). The loop later iterates the half-open range [in_str, in_str_end). str.size() here is the authoritative input length that also feeds the output-buffer allocation below, so the same length value governs both how many bytes are read and how big the destination is sized.

Fast path: if the string is empty (begin equals end, i.e. size 0) the function returns the original str unchanged, avoiding any allocation. This also short-circuits the size computation for the zero-length case.

Delimiter handling. If the caller supplied a non-empty delimiter, the first character of it is captured into `delim_char` and `quote_delim` is set true, so that occurrences of that character in the input will also be backslash-escaped in the default case below. charAt(0) is only reached after the delimiter.empty() guard, so the index-0 access is bounded. delim_char/quote_delim add at most one extra backslash per matching input byte.

The core sizing step. It reserves an output String whose capacity is `4 * str.size() + 1` bytes and grabs a writable pointer `out_str` into it via mutableData(). The factor 4 is the claimed worst-case expansion: the largest per-character output is the NUL case below, which emits 4 bytes (\, 0, 0, 0) for a single input byte; the +1 is for the trailing NUL terminator written at line 53. The comment asserts this is 'enough memory so that even if each character is quoted, we won't run out of room' — the entire memory-safety of the loop rests on that arithmetic being computed without overflow.

Sets up the copy loop with a read cursor `p` over the input range and a write cursor `q` over the reserved output buffer. Each iteration reads one input byte `c = *p` and appends one or more bytes at `q`, advancing `q` by the amount written. There is no bounds check on `q` inside the loop — the loop trusts that the line-21 reservation is large enough for every write, so correctness depends entirely on 4*size+1 being an accurate upper bound.

For each PCRE metacharacter (dot, backslash, plus, star, question mark, brackets, caret, dollar, parens, braces, equals, bang, angle brackets, pipe, colon, dash, hash) the function emits a backslash followed by the character itself — 2 output bytes per input byte. This is the standard regex-escaping expansion and stays within the 4x budget.

The NUL byte is escaped as the four-character sequence \000 (backslash, '0', '0', '0'). This is the maximum-expansion branch: one input byte becomes FOUR output bytes, which is exactly why the allocation multiplier at line 21 is 4. An input consisting entirely of NUL bytes therefore produces 4*size output bytes, saturating the reservation.

For any other byte, the character is copied verbatim (1 byte); if delimiter-quoting is active and this byte equals the delimiter character, a leading backslash is emitted first (2 bytes total). So the default branch writes at most 2 bytes per input byte, again within the 4x budget.

After the loop, a terminating NUL is written at the current write position (consuming the +1 reserved byte), and the String's logical length is set to the number of bytes actually produced, q - out_str (a pointer difference, i.e. the true output length). setSize finalizes the escaped string and it is returned. The final write at *q and the setSize length both assume q never advanced past the reserved capacity.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Integer overflow in the buffer-size computation. str.size() is attacker-controlled and, in the HHVM String API, is a machine int (signed 32-bit). The expression 4 * str.size() + 1 is evaluated in int arithmetic: as soon as str.size() exceeds roughly INT_MAX/4 (about 536,870,911, i.e. a ~512 MB string, which is within the runtime's allowed string size), the multiply overflows. Signed overflow is undefined behavior and in practice wraps to a small or negative value, so the String reserve constructor allocates a buffer FAR smaller than the 4*size bytes the loop can actually write (e.g. size = 0x20000000 makes 4*size wrap to 0, yielding a ~1-byte allocation). The invariant the implementation must uphold is that the reserved capacity is a true upper bound on the bytes the loop writes; that requires the size math to be done in a width that cannot overflow (size_t / 64-bit) AND a guard that rejects lengths where 4*size+1 would overflow, before allocating. This is the root defect (CWE-190).

Heap out-of-bounds write — the manifestation of the line-21 overflow. The loop writes 1 to 4 bytes per input byte through the unchecked cursor q, trusting the reservation. Once 4*str.size()+1 has wrapped to an undersized (or negative-turned-tiny) allocation, the loop keeps writing for every one of the str.size() input bytes and runs q far past the end of the heap allocation. With the maximal-expansion input (all NUL bytes, lines 39-44, 4 bytes each), the overflow reaches 4*size bytes written into a buffer sized as if it were only a handful of bytes. This is a classic remotely/userland-reachable heap buffer overflow (CWE-787) giving an attacker a large, content-controlled linear overwrite of adjacent heap memory — corruptable into control-flow hijack. The invariant: no write through q may occur beyond the reserved capacity; today nothing enforces that except the broken arithmetic.

Signedness / negative-size hazard, secondary to the same line. If the wrapped result of 4 * str.size() + 1 becomes negative and is passed to a String reserve constructor that takes a signed size, the allocator may treat it as a huge unsigned request (allocation failure / DoS) or a tiny/zero request (undersized buffer feeding the overflow above), depending on the constructor's parameter type and internal casts. Either way the value handed to the allocator no longer reflects the real output length. The implementation must validate that the computed capacity is positive and >= the maximum possible output before allocating.

Length finalization after a corrupted write. If the buffer was undersized due to the line-21 overflow, q has already advanced past the allocation before this point, so q - out_str reports a length larger than the buffer actually held; setSize then records an out-of-bounds logical length on top of memory that was already overwritten. This does not add a new primitive but shows the damage is committed into the returned String's metadata as well. The guard belongs upstream at the allocation, not here.

summary: This is HHVM's preg_quote() builtin. It contains a single, serious defect: the output buffer is reserved as `4 * str.size() + 1` (line 21) using signed int arithmetic on an attacker-controlled length, so a string larger than ~512 MB overflows the multiply to a small/negative value, under-allocates the destination, and the unchecked escaping loop then writes up to 4*size bytes past the end of the heap allocation — a content-controlled heap buffer overflow (CWE-190 root cause, CWE-787 impact). The escaping logic itself is otherwise correct (the 4x factor matches the worst-case \000 expansion).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: preg_quote(const String& str, const String& delimiter) signature (lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Both args are const references that outlive the call; the signature itself carries no unsafe contract — the risk is in how str.size() is used at line 21.

data() returns a pointer into str's internal buffer; str is a live const reference for the whole function, so the pointer is not dangling (the .data()/temporary use-after-free note does not apply here).
Correct one-past-end pointer; str.size() is a valid length. It only becomes dangerous where the same size feeds the overflowing multiply, tracked under line 21.
Correct zero-length early return; no security contract.
Only reached after !delimiter.empty(), so index 0 is in bounds; adds at most one extra backslash per input byte, within the 4x budget.
Attacker-controlled length multiplied by 4 in signed int arithmetic — integer overflow that under-allocates the output buffer (primary finding).
Writable pointer into the reserved buffer; safe only insofar as the reservation at line 21 is correctly sized — covered by that finding.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: copy loop writing through q without bounds check (lines 27-52)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unchecked writes of 1-4 bytes per input byte; becomes a heap out-of-bounds write once the line-21 allocation is undersized (CWE-787 manifestation).

This is the maximal 4-byte expansion that justifies the 4x multiplier; it is internally consistent and not a bug on its own — but it is the input that saturates/overflows the buffer, so it is the trigger for the line-21 finding.
Consumes the reserved +1 byte; safe when the reservation is correct, corrupted only as a downstream effect of the line-21 overflow.
Correct output-length computation via pointer difference; only reports a bogus length as a consequence of the line-21 overflow, not an independent defect.

findings.title: Integer overflow in preg_quote output-buffer size (4 * str.size() + 1) under-allocates the destination
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in size computation leading to heap under-allocation
findings.invariant: The reserved capacity must be a mathematically correct upper bound on the bytes the escaping loop can write (4*size + 1), computed in a width that cannot overflow for any allowed str.size(); lengths for which 4*size+1 would overflow must be rejected before allocation.
findings.failure_mode: str.size() is attacker-controlled and is a signed 32-bit int in the HHVM String API. For str.size() greater than ~INT_MAX/4 (~536,870,911, i.e. a ~512 MB PHP string, which the runtime permits), the expression 4 * str.size() + 1 overflows int and wraps to a small or negative value (e.g. size 0x20000000 -> 4*size wraps to 0). The String reserve constructor then allocates a buffer far smaller than needed, and the subsequent loop writes up to 4*size bytes into it, causing a large heap buffer overflow. Impact: attacker-controlled out-of-bounds heap write, exploitable to memory corruption / RCE, from a single preg_quote() call on a large userland string.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared type/width of String::size() (signed int vs size_t) and of the String(size, ReserveString) constructor's size parameter; verify the multiply is done in int and that no earlier guard rejects sizes where 4*size+1 overflows. Confirm the runtime's maximum allowed StringData size is >= INT_MAX/4 so the overflow is reachable.

findings.title: Heap out-of-bounds write in escaping loop when the reservation overflows
findings.anchor: for (p = in_str, q = out_str; p != in_str_end; p++) { ... *q++ = ...
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (consequence of the size overflow)
findings.invariant: No write through the cursor q (including the final *q = '\0' at line 53) may occur at or beyond out_str + reserved_capacity; the loop must never emit more bytes than the allocation guarantees.
findings.failure_mode: The loop performs *q++ writes with no bounds check, emitting 1-4 bytes per input byte (4 for the NUL case at lines 39-44). When line 21's reservation has wrapped to an undersized allocation, an all-NUL (or otherwise expansion-heavy) input of size N drives ~4*N byte writes into a buffer sized for only a handful of bytes, overwriting adjacent heap memory with attacker-controlled content. This is the concrete memory-corruption primitive produced by the CWE-190 root cause; it is listed separately because the write itself is the exploitable out-of-bounds operation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify there is no secondary capacity check inside the loop and that mutableData() returns exactly the (overflowed) reservation with no reallocation-on-write growth that would mask the bug; confirm the NUL-expansion branch is reachable with fully attacker-chosen bytes.

audit_checklist: Determine the exact integer type/width of String::size() and of the String(size, ReserveString) constructor parameter; if either is 32-bit signed, 4 * str.size() + 1 overflows for ~512 MB inputs.
Add an overflow-safe capacity computation (compute in size_t/64-bit and/or reject str.size() > (MAX-1)/4 before allocating) so the reservation is always >= the true maximum output length.
Confirm the runtime's maximum allowed string size is large enough that str.size() can exceed INT_MAX/4, establishing reachability of the overflow.

Verify the escaping loop's worst-case expansion is exactly 4 bytes/char (the \000 NUL case) so 4x is the right multiplier once overflow is fixed, and that no branch can emit more than 4 bytes per input byte.
Confirm the reserve buffer does not silently grow on out-of-bounds *q++ writes (no realloc-on-write), i.e. that an undersized reservation actually corrupts memory rather than being masked.
Check callers of preg_quote for any pre-existing input-length limits; absent one, treat arbitrarily large userland strings as reachable attacker input.
